ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lassi entry; banana lassi is a flavoured variant. Match is accepted as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fermented milk drink (lassi type) — banana flavoured variant estimated under fermented milk beverages category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for banana lassi exists. A0FKP (fermented milk-based drink) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor, representing a parent category. Banana lassi is a blended fermented milk beverage with fruit.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for banana lassi or lassi beverage category as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of banana lassi is approximately 300 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as commonly offered at Indian restaurants and food stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Lassi, mango (SR Legacy) and Banana, raw (FDC ID 1105314) used as component basis; ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) consulted for yogurt and banana reference values
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for banana lassi as a composite dish. Nutrient values were derived by weighted component estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 1097542), whole milk (FDC ID 1097512), ripe banana (FDC ID 1105314), and granulated sugar (FDC ID 1983); cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN published values for dahi (curd) and banana. Approximate blend ratio used: 45% yogurt, 30% milk, 20% banana, 5% sugar by weight. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with component-derived estimates and are within plausible range. Values rounded to two decimal places.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; banana (raw, ripe) GI ~51; plain yogurt GI ~36; blended lassi estimated composite GI ~52
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for banana lassi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated as a weighted composite: ripe banana GI 51 (Atkinson et al. 2021, University of Sydney GI Database), plain whole-milk yogurt GI 36 (University of Sydney GI Database), whole milk GI 31, sugar GI 65. Weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution per 100 g of final beverage (banana ~8 g carb, yogurt ~3 g carb, milk ~2 g carb, sugar ~5 g carb). Composite GI estimated at 52 (low category, GI < 55). Glycemic load per 300 g serving: (52 × 15.0 × 3) / 100 = 23.4 g. This is a medium GL serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage: sugar 12.67 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage threshold; added sugar independently raises blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form; an unsweetened version using only ripe banana and plain yogurt without added sugar would reduce sugar below the 5 g/100 g beverage gate and be more appropriate, as added sugar in liquid form is independently associated with elevated blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage: total sugar 12.67 g/100 g with dietary fibre only 0.67 g/100 g; liquid sugar absorbed rapidly with minimal glycaemic modulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form; removing added sugar entirely and relying only on ripe banana's natural sweetness would substantially reduce the glycaemic impact, and a 300 g serving of the sweetened version delivers approximately 23.4 g glycaemic load which is clinically significant for postprandial glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.67 g/100 g is low; however, added sugar at 12.67 g/100 g contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this sweetened beverage due to its high added sugar content (12.67 g/100 g), which is associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; an unsweetened version with plain yogurt and banana only would be more appropriate, as the low saturated fat (1.67 g/100 g) and potassium (~185 mg/100 g) from banana and yogurt are otherwise cardioprotective.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Yogurt provides live cultures supporting gut microbiome immune regulation; calcium 83.33 mg/100 g supports bone protection; however added sugar 12.67 g/100 g approaches pro-inflammatory threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the probiotic content of yogurt and the calcium from dairy components, but should reduce or eliminate added sugar to stay below the pro-inflammatory threshold; an unsweetened banana lassi with plain full-fat yogurt containing live cultures is preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Banana Lassi (केला लस्सी, 香蕉拉西, Lassi Pisang) is a traditional Indian chilled dairy beverage made by blending ripe bananas with plain yogurt (dahi), whole milk, and a small amount of sugar until smooth and creamy. It belongs to the lassi family of fermented milk drinks that originated in the Punjab region of the Indian subcontinent and is widely consumed across India and in Indian diaspora communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The drink has a thick, creamy texture with a naturally sweet banana flavour balanced by the mild tang of yogurt. It is typically served cold and is popular as a refreshing accompaniment to spicy meals, as a breakfast drink, or as a standalone snack. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 93 kcal, 3 g protein, 2.67 g fat (of which 1.67 g is saturated), and 15 g carbohydrate including 12.67 g sugar. It is a source of calcium (83 mg/100 g), potassium (~185 mg/100 g), vitamin B12, and riboflavin from the dairy components, and provides small amounts of vitamin C, vitamin B6, and dietary fibre from the banana. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low GI), but the glycemic load per standard 300 g glass is approximately 23.4 g (medium GL), making it clinically significant for individuals managing blood glucose. The high added sugar content (12.67 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage under clinical dietary guidelines, making it unsuitable in standard form for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. An unsweetened version using only ripe banana and plain yogurt without added sugar would be substantially more appropriate for these populations. The yogurt component provides live probiotic cultures beneficial for gut microbiome health and immune regulation, which is of particular relevance for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or inflammatory conditions.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for banana lassi as a composite dish. Nutrient values were derived by weighted component estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for plain whole-milk yogurt, whole milk, ripe banana, and granulated sugar, cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN published values. The input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with component-derived estimates. GI is an analogous composite estimate. Confidence is medium due to the absence of a direct approved-source match for the composite dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-derived estimates based on an approximate blend ratio of 45% plain whole-milk yogurt, 30% whole milk, 20% ripe banana, and 5% granulated sugar by weight, using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and ICMR-NIN reference values. No single approved-source entry exists for banana lassi as a composite dish. 2. vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed micronutrient data for this composite blended beverage are not available with sufficient precision in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN as of 2026; values for these nutrients in the component foods are highly variable and cannot be reliably aggregated without direct laboratory analysis. 3. GI of 52 is a weighted composite estimate; no direct GI measurement for banana lassi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. The ethnic_tags field in the input contained 'Butter Chicken' and 'Curry' which are irrelevant to banana lassi and have been removed. 5. Potassium (~185 mg/100 g), monounsaturated fat (0.69 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.10 g/100 g) are estimated from component sources and should be confirmed by direct laboratory analysis for production use. 6. All core macros (protein_g 3.0, fat_g 2.67, carbohydrate_g 15.0) are non-zero and consistent with a dairy-fruit blended beverage; no zero-macro justification is required.
